questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by two or three conclusions. Choose the correct Answer given below:
Statements:   C < H = J ,      X ≤ Y < J ,      N > X ≥ Z
 
Conclusions:  Y > Z ,      Y = Z
options: Both conclusions I and II follow
Either conclusion I or II follows
questions.solution:
Statements:   C < H = J ,      X ≤ Y < J ,      N > X ≥ Z
 
Conclusions:  Y > Z ,      Y = Z

For conclusion I: Y > Z

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ X ≥ Z

Here, the common sign between Y and Z is ‘≥’. Hence Y ≥ Z Thus conclusion I does not follow individually.

For conclusion II: Y = Z

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ X ≥ Z

Here, the common sign between Y and Z is ‘≥’. Hence Y ≥ Z. Thus conclusion II also does not follow individually.

On combining conclusions I and II, we get: Y ≥ Z, which is the true relationship.

Thus either conclusion I or II follows.

Hence option B is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In each question below are given some statements followed by some conclusions. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows/follow from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.
Statements:
All phones are tools.
Some tools are letters.
All letters are stars.
 
Conclusions:
All phones being stars is a possibility.
At least some letters are phones.
options: if both conclusion I and II follow
if neither conclusion I nor II follows
questions.solution:
Analytical Method:

Some tools are letters (I) + All letters are stars (A) = I + A = I = Some tools are stars (I).
 
Now, All phones are tools (A) + Some tools are stars (I) = A + I = No conclusion.
 
But the possibility in I exists.
 
Hence conclusion I follow.
 
Again, all phones are tools (A) + Some tools are letters (I) = A + I = No conclusion.
 
Hence, conclusion II does not follow.
 
Hence, option A is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by two conclusions. Choose the correct Answer given below:
Statements: R > I = N > P      Y ≥ R > K      N ≤ E < Z

Conclusions: K > I,         I < Z
options: Both conclusion I and II follow.
Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II follows.
questions.solution:
Statements: R > I = N > P      Y ≥ R > K      N ≤ E < Z

Conclusions: K > I,         I < Z
 
For conclusion I: K > I

From the statements I and II, we get:

I < R > K

Here, the signs on inequalities between I and R are getting reversed. Conclusion I hence doesn't follow.

For conclusion II: I < Z

Combining statements I and III, we get:

I = N ≤ E < Z

Here, the common sign between I and Z is ‘<’ and the given conclusion is also I < Z. Hence, conclusion II follows.

Hence, the correct answer is would be ‘Only conclusion II follows’.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Question : Directions: In each question below there are given some statements followed by some conclusions. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows/follow from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.
Statements:
No newspaper is magazine.
Not a single magazine is book.
Every book is story.
 
Conclusions:
I. Some stories which are books are newspapers as well.
II. No newspaper is a book.
III. Some stories are not magazines.
questions.solution:
Analytical Method:

Checking C1:
Some stories which are books are newspapers as well.
If we observe the given statements, we can find that in Statement 1 and 2, the middle term ‘magazine’ is distributed twice and therefore even after conversing either of the sentence we won’t be able to find a definite conclusion out of these two. Therefore, we can’t derive a definite relationship between ‘book’ and ‘newspaper’.
C1 hence doesn’t follow.

Checking C2:
No newspaper is a book.
Following the logic explained above, we can clearly say that C2 doesn’t follow either.

Checking C3:
Some stories are not magazines.
From S2 and S3,
Not a single magazine is book (E) + Every book is story (A) = Some stories are not magazines.
Clearly, C3 follows.

Among all, only C3 follows. Option A is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In this question, relationship between different elements is shown in the statements. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. The statement is followed by some conclusions. Study the conclusions based on the given statement and select the appropriate answer.
Statements:    C < L ≤ K = E ≤ Q < I,    J = Q < H ≤ S ≤ V > N,    A ≤ V < O = Y = W > U
 
Conclusions:    I. L < O,    II. S > K    III. O > C
questions.solution:
Statements:    C < L ≤ K = E ≤ Q < I,    J = Q < H ≤ S ≤ V > N,    A ≤ V < O = Y = W > U
 
Conclusions:    I. L < O,    II. S > K,    III. O > C
 
Combining both the equations to find the relationship between L and O, we get
 
L ≤ K = E ≤ Q < H ≤ S ≤ V < O
 
Clearly, the common sign of inequalities between L and O is of '<' and the given conclusion is L < O. C1, hence, follows.
 
Similarly, for S and K, we get
 
S ≥ H > Q ≥ E = K
 
Clearly, the common sign between S and K is of '>' and the given conclusion is S > K. C2, hence, follows as well.
 
Similarly, for O and C, we get
 
C < L ≤ K = E ≤ Q < H ≤ S ≤ V < O
 
Clearly, the common sign between C and O is of '<' , thus C < O  or O > C and the given conclusion is O > C. Hence C3, also follows.
 
Option D is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In this question, relationship between different elements is shown in the statements. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. The statement is followed by some conclusions. Study the conclusions based on the given statement and select the appropriate answer.
Statements:    T < H ≤ L < S ≤ K < A,    M = F ≠ S = G ≥ I > Q,    U ≤ B < N = C = I

Conclusions:    I. N < A,    II. T < B,    III. S ≥ N
questions.solution:
Statements:    T < H ≤ L < S ≤ K < A,    M = F ≠ S = G ≥ I > Q,    U ≤ B < N = C = I

Conclusions:    I. N < A,    II. T < B,    III. S ≥ N
 
Combining the equations to find the relationship between N and A, we get
 
N = C = I ≤ G = S ≤ K < A
 
Clearly, the common sign of inequalities between N and A is of '<'. Conclusion N < A is hence stays true. C1, hence, follows.
 
Similarly, combining equations to find the relationship between T and B, we get
 
T < H ≤ L < S = G ≥ I = C = N > B
 
Clearly, the signs are getting reversed and hence we can't define a relationship between T and B. C2, hence, doesn't follow.
 
Similarly, combining equations to find the relationship between S and N, we get
 
S = G ≥ I = C = N
 
Here the common sign between S and N is '≥' and the given conclusion is S ≥ N. Evidently , C3 follows.
 
Option B is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by two conclusions. Choose the correct Answer given below:
Statements:   T = K > L ,     D > K > U  ,    C = Z < T ,     F ≥ V > U
 
Conclusions:  U < T,    F = K,   C < L
options: Either conclusion I or II follows
Only conclusions II and III follow
questions.solution:
Statements:   T = K > L ,     D > K > U  ,    C = Z < T ,     F ≥ V > U
 
Conclusions:  U < T,    F = K,   C < L

For conclusion I: U < T

From statements I and II, we get:

T = K > U

Here, the common sign between T and U is '>'. Thus T > U or U < T.

Hence conclusion I follows.

For conclusion II: F = K

From statements II and IV, we get:

K > U < V ≤ F

Here, we can see the opposite signs between K and F. Thus no relation can be established between them.

Hence conclusion II does not follow.

For conclusion III: C < L

From statements I, II and III, we get:

Z = C < T = K > L

Here, we can see the opposite signs between C and D. Thus no relation can be established between them.

Hence conclusion III does not follow.

Therefore only conclusion I follows.

Hence option A is correct.
.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions : In question, some statements are given, followed by two conclusions I and II. You have to consider the statements to be true, even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. You have to decide which of the given conclusions, if any, follow from the given statements. Indicate your answer.
Statements :
All radios are electric goods.
All table-lamps are electric goods.
 
Conclusions :
I. Some radios are table-lamps.
II. Some table-lamps are radios.
options: If either I or II follows.
If neither I nor II follows.
questions.solution:
Checking C1 : Some radios are table-lamps.
 
We can observe that the class 'radios' and 'table-lamps' are present in different statements and the middle term 'electric goods' is not being distributed even once in either of the statements. Therefore, no definite relationship is possible between these two classes. C1, doesn't follow.
 
Checking C2 : Some table-lamps are radios.
 
Following the same logic discussed above, we can state that C2 doesn't follow either.
 
Hence, option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Question : Directions: In each question below there are given some statements followed by some conclusions. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. Read all the conclusions and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows/follow from the given statements, disregarding commonly known facts.
Statements:
Some hours are minutes.
All clocks are minutes.
No minute is second.

Conclusions:
I. Some hours are clocks.
II. All seconds being clocks is a possibility.
III. No hour is a clocks.
questions.solution:
Analytical Method:

Checking C1 and C3:
‘Some hours are clocks’ and ‘No hour is a clocks.’

In S1 and S2, we can observe that the middle term ‘clocks’ is not distributed even once, a definite conclusion can’t be derived between these two.  And C1 is an I type statement and C2 is an E type statement, they both form a complementary pair (E + I combination).

Therefore, either C1 or C3 follows.

Checking C2:
All seconds being clocks is a possibility.

From S2 and S3,

All clocks are minutes + No minute is second = No clock is a second.

Clearly, there is no possibility that exists between ‘second’ and 'clock’.

C2 hence doesn’t follow.

Evidently, either C1 or C3 follows.

Clearly, option B is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Study the following question carefully and choose the right answer:
Statements: T ≤ M ≤ J = Q > F > S; M > R


Conclusions: I. Q ≥ T                  II. J > S
options: Either conclusion I or II is true
Both conclusion I and II are true
questions.solution:
Statements: T ≤ M ≤ J = Q > F > S ...(i) 
                      M > R ...(ii) 
 
From (i), we get
 
T ≤ Q or Q ≥ T. Thus, conclusion I is true.
 
Again, J > S is true. Hence both conclusion I and II are true.

Hence, option (D) is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In this question, relationship between different elements is shown in the statements. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. The statement is followed by some conclusions. Study the conclusions based on the given statement and select the appropriate answer.
Statement:    A = B = C ≥ D ≥ E ≥ F = G = H
 
Conclusions:    I. C > F,    II. B = G,    III. H ≤ D
options: Either C1 or C2 and C3 follows
questions.solution:
Given Statement:    A = B = C ≥ D ≥ E ≥ F = G = H
 
For C1, we can see that between C and F the common sign is of '≥' which means C1 individually doesn't follow. But if we look at C2 which is B = G (or C = F because we have B = C and G = F), we can say that either C1 or C2 follows.
 
Now, between H and D the common sign is of '≤' (while moving from H to D), which is given as C3. 
 
Hence C3 completely follows.
 
Option D is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions : In question, some statements are given, followed by two conclusions I and II. You have to consider the statements to be true, even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. You have to decide which of the given conclusions, if any, follow from the given statements. Indicate your answer.
Statements :
No man is a monkey.
John is a man.
 
Conclusions :
I. John is not a monkey.
II. John may be a monkey.
options: If either I or II follows.
If neither I nor II follows.
questions.solution:
Checking C1 : John is not a monkey.
 
Using S2 and S1, we get
 
John is a man (A) + No man is a monkey (E) ⇒ John is not a monkey. Clearly, C1 follows.
 
Checking C2 : John may be a monkey.
 
In C1, we have already found that John is not a monkey is a definite conclusion and therefore John may be a monkey is not a possible preposition. C2, hence, doesn't follow.
 
Option A is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions : In question, some statements are given, followed by two conclusions I and II. You have to consider the statements to be true, even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. You have to decide which of the given conclusions, if any, follow from the given statements. Indicate your answer.
Statements : 
No dancers are actors.
Some actors are artists.
No artist is artisan.
 
Conclusions : 
I. Some artists are not dancers.
II. Some artisans are not actors.
options: If neither conclusion I nor conclusion II follows
questions.solution:
Checking Conclusion I : Some artists are not dancers.
 
Some artists are actors (Converse of S2) + No actor is dancer (Converse of S1) = Some artists are not dancers. Clearly, C1 follows.
 
Checking Conclusion II : Some artisans are not actors.
 
Some actors are artists + No artist is artisan = Some actors are not artisans. Since converse of an O type statement is not possible, C2 doesn't follow.
 
Option A is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

Conclusions: K > I,         I < Z
options: Both conclusion I and II follow.

questions.solution:
Statements: R > I = N > P      Y ≥ R > K      N ≤ E < Z

Conclusions: K > I,         I < Z
 
For conclusion I: K > I

questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by three conclusions. Choose the correct answer on the basis of information given below.
Statements:   Y < X < W ,      Y < I > L ,     C = F < L ,    S > D > C  
 
Conclusions:  W < I ,     S > F   ,    I > C
options: Either conclusion I or III follows
Only conclusions II and III follow
questions.solution:
Statements:   Y < X < W ,      Y < I > L ,     C = F < L ,    S > D > C  
 
Conclusions:  W < I ,     S > F   ,    I > C

For conclusion I: W < I

From statements I and II, we get:

I > Y < W

Here, we get the opposite signs between W and I. Thus no relation can be established between them.

Hence conclusion I does not follow.

For conclusion II: S > F

From statements III and IV, we get:

S > C = F

Here, the common sign between S and F is '>'. Thus S > F.

Hence conclusion II follows.

For conclusion III: I > C

From statements II and III, we get:

I > L > C = F

Here, the common sign between I and C is '>'. Thus I > C.

Hence conclusion III follows.

Since only conclusions II and III follow.

Hence option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by two conclusions. Choose the correct Answer given below:
Statements: H > K = O > R      K ≥ M > L        O ≤ F < Y
 
Conclusions: F > R,      M < H
options: Both conclusion I and II follow.
Neither conclusion I nor conclusion II follows.
questions.solution:
Statements: H > K = O > R      K ≥ M > L        O ≤ F < Y
 
Conclusions: F > R,      M < H

For conclusion I: F > R

Combining statements I and III, we get:
F ≥ O > R

Here, the common sign between F and R is ‘>’ and the given conclusion is F > R. Hence, conclusion I follows.

For conclusion II: M < H

Combining statements I and II, we get:

H > K ≥ M

Here, the common sign between H and M is ‘>’ and the given conclusion is M < H. Conclusion II follows.

Hence, the correct answer is would be ‘Both the statements I and II follow’.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

Conclusions:  I. L > N                      II. R ≥ P
options: If only conclusion I is true
If only conclusion II is true
If either conclusion I or II is true
If both conclusions I and II are true
questions.solution:
Given Statement:          L ≥ Q > P = N ≤ K ≤ R

Thus, L > N is true.
Again, P ≤ R or R ≥ P is true. Hence, both I and II are true.

questions.q:
Directions: Study the following questions carefully and choose the right answer:
Statements:
Some trees are plants.
All bushes are plants.
 
Conclusions:
I. At least some trees are bushes.
II. Some trees are definitely not bushes.
options: if either conclusion I or conclusion II follows.
if neither conclusion I nor conclusion II follows.
questions.solution:
Venn Diagram Method:
 

Analytical Method:

All bushes are plants + Some plants are trees = A + I = No conclusion.
 
Hence, neither conclusion I nor conclusion II follows.
 
Hence, option D is correct.

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions : In question, some statements are given, followed by two conclusions I and II. You have to consider the statements to be true, even if they seem to be at variance from commonly known facts. You have to decide which of the given conclusions, if any, follow from the given statements. Indicate your answer.
 
Statements :
Only fantastic are fans.
No fans are fabulous.
 
Conclusions :
I. No fantastic being fabulous is a possibility. 
II. Some fabulous are fantastic.
options: If both conclusion I and II follow.
If neither conclusion I nor II follows.
questions.solution:
Statements :
Only fantastic are fans.
No fans are fabulous.
 
Conclusions :
I. No fantastic being fabulous is a possibility. 
II. Some fabulous are fantastic.

Checking C1: No fantastic being fabulous is a possibility. 

From S1, 'Only fantastic are fans' we get 'All fans are fantastic' but 'fans can't be others'.

From S2, we get that 'No fans are fabulous' which implies that no element of class 'fabulous' exists in class 'fans'. Thus there is a possibility that no element of class 'fabulous' exists in class 'fantastic' either. Thus, C1 follows.

Checking C2: Some fabulous are fantastic.

As per the explanation above, it's a possibility and not a surety. Thus, C2 doesn't follow. 

Option A is hence the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Study the following questions carefully and choose the right answer given beside.
Which of the following symbols should replace the question mark in the given statement in order to make conclusion 'W = O' as well as ' P > K' definitely true?

L > W = P ? O ≥ T > K
questions.solution:
In order to make the given conclusions ' W = O' and ' P > K' definitely true, we have to apply all the given symbols in the options one by one.

For option A: >

L > W = P > O ≥ T > K

Here, the common sign between W and O is '>', thus it does not follow W = O.

The common sign between P and K is also '>' , thus it follows P > K.

Since, we need a symbol which will follow both the conclusions simultaneously, option A gets eliminated.

For option B: <

L > W = P < O ≥ T > K

Here, the common sign between W and O is '<', thus it does not follow W = O.

Here, there are opposite signs between P and K . Thus, no relationship can be established between them.

Hence, it does not follow both the conclusions.

For option C: ≥

L > W = P ≥ O ≥ T > K

Here, the common sign between W and O is '≥', thus it does not follow W = O solely.

Here, the common sign between P and K is '>', thus it follows P > K.

Since, we need a symbol which will follow both the conclusions simultaneously, option C gets eliminated.

For option D: =

L > W = P = O ≥ T > K

Here, the common sign between W and O is '=', thus it follows W = O.

Here, the common sign between P and K is '>', thus it follows P > K.

Since both the conclusions follow simultaneously by using '=' symbol.

Hence option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

Directions: Study the following question carefully and choose the right answer:
Statement:     B ≤ D = E > G ≥ H ≥ J

Conclusions:   I.  E > B                     II. D > J
options: If only conclusion I is true
If only conclusion II is true
If either conclusion I or II is true
If neither conclusion I nor II is true
questions.solution:
Given Statement:     B ≤ D = E > G ≥ H ≥ J

Thus, B ≤ E or E ≥ B. It means E > B may be true or E = B may be true. Hence, I (E > B) is not true. Again, D > J is true. Hence II is true.

questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: Study the following questions carefully and choose the right answer given beside.
Which of the following symbols should replace the question mark in the given statement in order to make conclusion 'W = F' as well as ' P > Y' definitely true?
 
M > W = P ? F ≥ T > Y
questions.solution:
In order to make the given conclusions ' W = F' and ' P > Y' definitely true, we have to apply all the given symbols in the options one by one.

For option A: >

M > W = P > F ≥ T > Y

Here, the common sign between W and F is '>', thus it does not follow W = F.

The common sign between P and Y is also '>' , thus it follows P > Y.

Since, we need a symbol which will follow both the conclusions simultaneously, option A gets eliminated.

For option B: <

M > W = P < F ≥ T > Y

Here, the common sign between W and F is '<', thus it does not follow W = F.

Here, there are opposite signs between P and Y . Thus, no relationship can be established between them.

Hence, it does not follow both the conclusions.

For option C: ≥

M > W = P ≥ F ≥ T > Y

Here, the common sign between W and F is '≥', thus it does not follow W = F solely.

Here, the common sign between P and Y is '>', thus it follows P > Y.

Since, we need a symbol which will follow both the conclusions simultaneously, option C gets eliminated.

For option D: =

M > W = P = F ≥ T > Y

Here, the common sign between W and F is '=', thus it follows W = F.

Here, the common sign between P and Y is '>', thus it follows P > Y.

Since both the conclusions follow simultaneously by using '=' symbol.

Hence option D is correct.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.examName: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
questions.q:
Directions: In these questions, relationship between different elements is shown in the statement. The statements are followed by two conclusions. Choose the correct Answer given below:
Statements:   C < H = J ,      X ≤ Y < J ,      N > X ≥ Z
 
Conclusions:  Y > Z ,      Y = Z
options: Both conclusions I and II follow
Either conclusion I or II follows
questions.solution:
Statements:   C < H = J ,      X ≤ Y < J ,      N > X ≥ Z
 
Conclusions:  Y > Z ,      Y = Z

For conclusion I: Y > Z

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ X ≥ Z

Here, the common sign between Y and Z is ‘≥’. Hence Y ≥ Z Thus conclusion I does not follow individually.

For conclusion II: Y = Z

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ X ≥ Z

Here, the common sign between Y and Z is ‘≥’. Hence Y ≥ Z. Thus conclusion II also does not follow individually.

On combining conclusions I and II, we get: Y ≥ Z, which is the true relationship.

Statements:  A < B, C = D , E < F, B > D, G ≥ C, A > F, H = E

Conclusions:  G > B    A > H    C = H

By combining all the statements, we get the following equation:

G ≥ C = D < B > A > F > E = H

For conclusion (I): G > B

Here we can see the opposite signs between G and B, thus no relationship can be established between them.

Hence conclusion (I) does not follow.

For conclusion (II): A > H

Here, the common sign between A and H is '>'. Thus A > H.

Thus conclusion (II) follows.
 
For conclusion (III): C = H

Therefore conclusion (III) does not follow.

Hence option C is correct.

Statements: H > K = O > R      K ≥ M > L        O ≤ F < Y

For conclusion II: M < H

questions.q:
Directions: Study the following questions carefully and choose the right answer given beside.
In which of the following expressions 'W > Z' as well as ' Y > Z' hold definitely true?
options: W > X > U = Z < H ≤ Y
W ≥ X = U > Z ≤ H < Y
W = X > U ≥ Z < H < Y
questions.solution:
In order to ensure the statement that holds ' W > Z' and ' Y > Z' definitely true, we have to check all the options one by one.

For option A:

W > X > U = Z < H ≤ Y

Here, the common sign between W and Z is '>', thus it follows W > Z.

The common sign between Z and Y is also '<' , thus it follows Z < Y or Y > Z.


For option B:

W ≥ X = U > Z ≤ H < Y

Here also, the common sign between W and Z is '>', thus it follows W > Z.

The common sign between Z and Y is also '<' , thus it follows Z < Y or Y > Z.

For option C: 

W = X > U ≥ Z < H < Y
 
Here again, the common sign between W and Z is '>', thus it follows W > Z.

The common sign between Z and Y is also '<' , thus it follows Z < Y or Y > Z.

Since all the three expressions hold the given conclusions true, option D "all of the above" is correct.

Hence option D is correct.

Hence option A is correct.

questions.q:
Directions: In this question, relationship between different elements is shown in the statements. You have to take the given statements to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts. The statement is followed by some conclusions. Study the conclusions based on the given statement and select the appropriate answer.
Statements:    W < U = V ≤ X,    V > Y ≥ Z,    Y < P ≤ Q = R

Conclusions:    I. W > Z,    II. Z < R
options: Either conclusion I or conclusion II follows.
questions.solution:
Only conclusion II follows
 
Option B, is hence the correct answer.
 
Common explanation:
 
There is a priority order of the symbols according to which the priority of the symbol is decided to reach the conclusion. Whenever there are two or more type of symbols are there between two objects whose relation is to be determine we have to use this order.
 
If the direction of sign is opposite between that two objects, no relation can be stated perfectly and we have to say that it does not follow.
 
Priority Order:
1.      > and < 
2.      ≥ and  ≤
3.           =
 
For conclusion I: W > Z
 
Combining statement I and II:
 
W < U = V > Y ≥ Z
 
As, the sign between W and Z are in opposite direction so, the chain breaks, hence we cannot conclude the relation between W and Z.
 
Hence, the conclusion does not follow.
 
For conclusion II: Z < R
 
Combining statement II and III:
 
Z ≤ Y < P ≤ Q = R
 
As, “<” sign is the highest prior sign here, so, Z < R is the actual relation.
 
Hence, the conclusion follows.
 
Only conclusion II follows

Hence, option B is correct.

Conclusions:    I. L ≤ H,    II. S < M,    III. J < Z

L ≤ M = E ≤ X < H

Clearly, the common sign between S and M is of '>' and the given conclusion is S < M. C2, hence, does not follow.
 
Similarly, for J and Z, we get
 
J = X < H ≤ S ≤ V < T = Z

Option B is hence the correct answer.

While combining conclusion I i.e. Z < Y and conclusion III i.e. Z = Y, we'll get Z ≤ Y, which is the actual relationship between them.

Conclusions:  Y > R ,    M < Z,     Y = R
options: Both conclusions I and III follow
Either conclusion I or III follows
questions.solution:
Statements:   X = V ≤ Y ,    Y ≥ Z < C,   X ≥ P = R,    C = M > O
 
Conclusions:  Y > R ,    M < Z,     Y = R

For Conclusion I: Y > R

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ V = X ≥ P = R

Here, the common sign between Y and R is '>' . Thus Y ≥ R.

Hence conclusion I does not follow individually.

For Conclusion II: M < Z

From statements II and IV, we get:

Z < C = M

Here, we the common sign between Z and  M is '<'. Thus Z < M or M > Z.

Hence conclusion II does not follow.

For Conclusion III: Y = R

From statements I and III, we get:

Y ≥ V = X ≥ P = R

Here, the common sign between Y and R is '>' . Thus Y ≥ R.

Hence conclusion III does not follow individually.

Combining conclusion I and III
 
 Since conclusion I is that "Y > R" and conclusion III is that "Y = R" and we have the true relationship as "Y ≥ R", so if we combine both the conclusions, we will arrive at the conclusion that Y is either equal to or greater than R i.e. Y ≥ R.

Thus either conclusion I or III follows.

Hence option B is correct.

Directions: Study the following questions carefully and choose the right answer given beside.
In which of the following expressions 'L > P' as well as ' J > P' hold definitely true?
options: L > X > E = P < H ≤ J
L ≥ X = E > P ≤ H < J
L = X > E ≥ P < H < J
questions.solution:
In order to ensure the statement that holds ' L > P' and ' J > P' definitely true, we have to check all the options one by one.

For option A:

L > X > E = P < H ≤ J

Here, the common sign between L and P is '>', thus it follows L > P.

The common sign between P and J is also '<' , thus it follows P < J or J > P.


For option B:

L ≥ X = E > P ≤ H < J

Here also, the common sign between L and P is '>', thus it follows L > P.

The common sign between P and J is also '<' , thus it follows P < J or J > P.

For option C: 

L = X > E ≥ P < H < J
 
Here again, the common sign between L and P is '>', thus it follows L > P.

The common sign between P and J is also '<' , thus it follows P < J or J > P.

Since all the three expressions hold the given conclusions true, option D "all of the above" is correct.

Hence option D is correct.

questions.q:
Directions: In each question three statements followed by two conclusions numbered I and II have been given. You have to take the given statement to be true even if they seem to be at variance with commonly known facts and then decide which of the given conclusions logically follows from the given statements. Give answer.
Statements :
All frocks are pants.
100% shorts are frocks.
Some jackets are pants.
 
Conclusions :
I. All pants are shorts.
II. All shorts being jackets is a possibility.
options: If neither conclusion I nor II follows.
If both conclusion I and II follows.
questions.solution:
Statements:
All frocks are pants.
100% shorts are frocks.
Some jackets are pants.
 
Conclusions:
I. All pants are shorts.
II. All shorts being jackets is a possibility.

Checking C1: All pants are shorts.

Using S2 and S1, we get

'100% shorts are frocks' or 'All shorts are frocks' + 'All frocks are pants' = All shorts are pants. Clearly, C1 doesn't follow. 

Checking C2: All shorts being jackets is a possibility.

As there is no negative statement, possibility between 'shorts' and 'jackets' does exist. C2, hence, follows.

Evidently, option B is the correct answer.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide

questions.q:
Directions: Study the following question carefully and choose the right answer:
Statements: T < M < J = Q ≥ F > S; M > R 
 
Conclusions: I. M < S                II. F ≥ R
options: Either conclusion I or II is true
Neither conclusion I nor II are true
questions.solution:
Statements:  T < M < J = Q ≥ F > S ...(i) 
                      M > R ...(ii) 
 
Combining (i) and (ii), we get
 
R < M < J = Q ≥ F 
 
Thus, we can't compare F and R. Hence II (F ≥ R) is not true. Again, we can't compare M and S. Hence I (M < S) is not true.
questions.source: English and Logical Reasoning Prep Guide
